title: The architecture of being noticed
summary: Argue that cities physically and procedurally distribute visibility, and that this distribution is unjust.
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay claiming that the architecture of cities — pavements, plazas, lobbies, signage — does not merely reflect who is already noticed but actively rewards visibility on unfair terms. Use specific international examples (Paris, Mumbai, São Paulo, Seoul, or others you know). Deploy inversion at least once, at least one cleft, and complex concession ('granted… even so…'). No moralising lists. No TED-Talk opener.
hintStarters: Rarely does a city advertise the rules by which…
What is striking about the modern plaza is not its openness but…
Granted, no urban planner sets out to…
There is a temptation to read visibility as merit, but…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đường phố lớn, lối qua lại
không có ma sát, trơn tru

sampleText:
Rarely does a city advertise the rules by which it grants attention. Yet anyone who has stood for a few minutes outside the Galeries Lafayette on Boulevard Haussmann, or near the entrance of the Trump Tower atrium on Fifth Avenue, will have felt them at work. The pavement narrows; the frontage widens; a uniformed doorman moves a velvet rope half a metre to the left. A camera is mounted above the awning. Without anyone saying anything, one set of bodies becomes visible — coats catalogued by the glass, gait studied by the staff — while another set, the courier, the hawker, the older woman dragging a shopping trolley, is gently demoted into the background.

This is the architecture of being noticed. It does not begin with character or merit; it begins with frontage, sightline, and the depth of the set-back from the kerb. In Mumbai, the brokers who matter are the ones whose offices face the sea at Nariman Point — not because the sea improves their judgement, but because the address pre-loads their visibility. In Seoul, certain Gangnam plazas have been engineered with a gentle slope toward the entrance of luxury brands, so that the pedestrian flow tilts, almost imperceptibly, toward the shops that pay the highest ground-floor rent. In São Paulo, the helipad — and the private route from helipad to boardroom — exists precisely so that one class of person can be seen by exactly the people they wish to be seen by, and by no one else.

What is striking about the modern plaza is not its openness but the way that openness is rationed. Public space, ostensibly permeable, is in practice scored. Benches are tilted to discourage sleeping; lighting is dimmed in some corners and intensified in others; signage in English greets one kind of traveller and is silent for another. The vestibule of a five-star hotel in Doha is more legible to a guest than the bus shelter outside is to a domestic worker waiting in heat — and the legibility is the point. To be noticed in such a space, one must already match the grammar the space was built to read.

Granted, no urban planner sets out to be cruel. The decisions are usually procedural: a permit here, a frontage rule there, a security review that quietly converts a public concourse into a privately patrolled one. Even so, the cumulative effect is a kind of moral sorting at the level of infrastructure. Visibility, which one might have hoped to be roughly democratic, turns out to be infrastructurally underwritten. It is the ground-floor rent, the camera angle, the doorman's discretion — not the citizen's worth — that tacitly decides whose presence the city registers.

There is a temptation to read visibility as merit. The well-dressed man crossing the lobby of the Mandarin Oriental in Hong Kong looks, from a distance, as though he has been noticed because he is worth noticing. It would seem that the city sees him because he matters. The truer story is the inverse: the city has been built, over decades, to see precisely him, and to look past the man with the toolbox standing four metres behind him. What we are reading as recognition is, in part, an effect of the architecture itself.

It is not that the unnoticed are absent; it is that the city has been instructed, by its own design choices, to file them as background. To insist that this is unjust is not to demand a flattening of difference — different rooms will, reasonably, do different work. It is to demand that the moral cost be acknowledged. A city that grants attention only along the lines of frontage and uniform is one that has outsourced part of its conscience to its own pavements. That outsourcing, one might argue, is what most needs to be brought back indoors — into the planning meeting, the permit hearing, the rent calculation — where someone can be asked to answer for it.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion for rhetorical opening
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở 'Rarely does a city advertise the rules by which it grants attention.' đảo trợ động từ 'does' lên trước chủ ngữ sau trạng từ phủ định 'Rarely' — một kĩ thuật C1 để khởi đầu trang trọng và có trọng lượng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening 'Rarely does a city advertise the rules by which it grants attention.' inverts the auxiliary 'does' before the subject after the negative adverb 'Rarely' — a C1 move that gives the opening formal weight.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Complex concession with 'Granted… Even so…'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'Granted, no urban planner sets out to be cruel… Even so, the cumulative effect is a kind of moral sorting…' công nhận điểm yếu của lập luận đối lập rồi lật lại — đây là cách nhường-bước-rồi-phản-biện điển hình ở trình độ C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The pattern 'Granted, no urban planner sets out to be cruel… Even so, the cumulative effect is a kind of moral sorting…' concedes the opposing point and then turns it — a textbook C1 concede-and-counter move.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi một thành phố công khai các quy tắc mà nó dùng để ban phát sự chú ý. Nhưng bất cứ ai đã đứng vài phút trước cửa Galeries Lafayette trên đại lộ Haussmann, hoặc gần lối vào sảnh Trump Tower trên Đại lộ Năm, đều cảm nhận được chúng đang vận hành. Vỉa hè hẹp lại; mặt tiền mở rộng ra; một nhân viên gác cửa mặc đồng phục dịch sợi dây nhung sang trái nửa mét. Một chiếc máy quay được lắp phía trên mái hiên. Không cần ai nói gì, một nhóm người trở nên hiện diện — áo khoác được kính ghi nhận, dáng đi được nhân viên quan sát — trong khi nhóm khác, anh giao hàng, người bán rong, bà cụ kéo xe đẩy đi chợ, bị nhẹ nhàng đẩy về phía hậu cảnh.

Đó là kiến trúc của sự được nhìn thấy. Nó không bắt đầu từ phẩm cách hay năng lực; nó bắt đầu từ mặt tiền, đường tầm nhìn, và độ lùi của toà nhà so với lề đường. Ở Mumbai, các nhà môi giới có trọng lượng là những người mà văn phòng nhìn ra biển ở Nariman Point — không phải vì biển làm họ phán đoán tốt hơn, mà vì địa chỉ ấy đã 'nạp sẵn' tính hiện diện cho họ. Ở Seoul, một số quảng trường tại Gangnam được thiết kế hơi dốc về phía lối vào của các thương hiệu xa xỉ, để dòng người đi bộ nghiêng — gần như không nhận thấy — về phía những cửa hàng trả tiền thuê tầng trệt cao nhất. Ở São Paulo, sân đáp trực thăng — và lối đi riêng từ sân đáp tới phòng họp — tồn tại chính vì lẽ ấy: để một tầng lớp người có thể được nhìn thấy bởi đúng những người mà họ muốn được nhìn thấy, và không ai khác.

Điều đáng chú ý về quảng trường hiện đại không phải là sự cởi mở, mà là cách sự cởi mở đó được chia khẩu phần. Không gian công cộng, bề ngoài có vẻ thông thoáng, trên thực tế được chấm điểm. Ghế đá nghiêng để ngăn người nằm; ánh sáng giảm ở góc này và tăng ở góc kia; biển hiệu tiếng Anh chào đón một loại khách và im lặng với loại khác. Tiền sảnh khách sạn năm sao ở Doha dễ đọc với khách hơn là trạm xe buýt phía ngoài đối với người giúp việc đợi giữa nắng — và sự dễ đọc đó chính là điểm.

Cứ cho là không nhà quy hoạch đô thị nào đặt mục tiêu tàn nhẫn. Quyết định thường mang tính thủ tục: một giấy phép ở đây, một quy định mặt tiền ở kia, một đợt rà soát an ninh âm thầm biến lối đi công cộng thành lối đi do tư nhân canh gác. Dù vậy, hệ quả tích luỹ là một dạng phân loại đạo đức ở cấp hạ tầng. Tính hiện diện, vốn ta hi vọng tương đối dân chủ, hoá ra được ngầm bảo đảm bởi hạ tầng.

Có một khuynh hướng đọc sự hiện diện như một phẩm chất. Người đàn ông ăn mặc lịch lãm bước qua sảnh khách sạn Mandarin Oriental ở Hồng Kông, nhìn từ xa, có vẻ được chú ý vì xứng đáng được chú ý. Câu chuyện đúng hơn là ngược lại: thành phố đã được xây, qua nhiều thập niên, để nhìn thấy đúng anh ta.

Không phải những người vô hình không có ở đó; mà thành phố, qua chính các lựa chọn thiết kế của mình, đã được dạy cách xếp họ vào hậu cảnh. Đòi hỏi điều này là bất công không phải là đòi xoá nhoà khác biệt — các phòng khác nhau, hợp lí thay, làm những việc khác nhau. Đó là đòi hỏi cái giá đạo đức phải được công nhận.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses inversion most naturally at C1 register?
options: A city rarely advertises the rules by which it grants attention.
Rarely does a city advertise the rules by which it grants attention.
Rarely a city advertises the rules of attention granting.
It is rare that a city is advertising the rules that grants attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: After a negative adverb like 'Rarely' at the start of a sentence, the auxiliary inverts: 'Rarely does a city…'.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative adverb up front triggers do/does/did inversion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the most effective cohesion device to begin a concessive paragraph?
options: In addition,
Granted, no urban planner sets out to be cruel.
Furthermore, urban planners exist.
On the bright side,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' opens a concession and prepares the reader for an 'even so' turn.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the move that concedes the opposing point first.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best matches C1 register for the essay's claim?
options: Cities are bad at being fair to poor people, full stop.
Visibility, which one might have hoped to be roughly democratic, turns out to be infrastructurally underwritten.
Visibility is unfair because rich people get noticed more.
Some people are seen and some are not, which is the way it is.
mcQuestions.explanation: B nominalises 'visibility', hedges with 'one might have hoped', and uses 'infrastructurally underwritten' — all C1 hallmarks.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for nominalization and a hedged main verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a cleft to focus on the agent of decision?
options: It is the ground-floor rent, the camera angle, the doorman's discretion — not the citizen's worth — that tacitly decides whose presence the city registers.
Ground-floor rent and angle and discretion decides presence.
The city registers presence and rents and discretion together.
Whose presence the city registers, it is tacitly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X — not Y — that …' is a cleft with a parenthetical contrast, pushing the real agent to the front.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is … that …' framing the real subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing avoids AI-style filler and lands the argument?
options: In conclusion, cities should be more equal places to live.
Throughout history, urban planners have shaped our lives.
That outsourcing, one might argue, is what most needs to be brought back indoors — into the planning meeting, the permit hearing, the rent calculation — where someone can be asked to answer for it.
It is important to note that visibility matters for everyone.
mcQuestions.explanation: C uses hedging ('one might argue'), a three-part em-dashed gloss, and a concrete relocation of moral responsibility.
mcQuestions.hint: The right answer is concrete and hedged, not slogan-like.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to: 'the city has been instructed, by its own design choices, to file them as background'?
options: The city has been taught by its design to treat them as background.
The city designed people to look like a background.
Design choices file the city as background.
Background people instruct the city to design itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps the passive frame and the agent ('design choices') while replacing 'instructed' and 'file' with simpler near-synonyms.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the paraphrase that preserves passive voice and the same agent.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition fits a paragraph that turns from concession to claim?
options: Granted, no urban planner sets out to be cruel. Even so, the cumulative effect is a kind of moral sorting.
Granted urban planners are cruel and therefore the effect is sorting.
Furthermore, planners do not intend harm, therefore the effect is fine.
On the other hand, planners are fine and the effect is also fine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted … Even so …' is the canonical concede-and-counter pair.
mcQuestions.hint: Two-step move: concede first, then turn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which item shows the best lexical precision for an essay about city design?
options: Big things in the city like buildings and stuff.
Frontage, sightline, and the depth of the set-back from the kerb.
Walls, doors, and other building parts that are important.
Stores, plazas, and big city architecture features.
mcQuestions.explanation: Specific architectural nouns ('frontage', 'sightline', 'set-back', 'kerb') signal C1 precision.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the best near-paraphrase of 'the legibility is the point'?
options: Being easy to read is exactly what matters here.
Legibility is unimportant and beside the point.
Reading legibility is one of many points.
The point is illegible from a distance.
mcQuestions.explanation: A captures the rhetorical force: legibility is not a side effect; it is the design intent.
mcQuestions.hint: The phrase emphasises intent, not accident.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the best closing transition for the penultimate paragraph?
options: What we are reading as recognition is, in part, an effect of the architecture itself.
What we are reading is recognition because the architecture is good.
Architecture and recognition are very different things to read.
The architecture itself reads us, partly in effect.
mcQuestions.explanation: A hedges ('in part') and reframes the reader's intuition without overstating.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best preserves cohesion when introducing a counter-example?
options: In São Paulo, the helipad — and the private route from helipad to boardroom — exists precisely so that one class of person can be seen by exactly the people they wish to be seen by, and by no one else.
São Paulo also has helipads which are private boardroom routes.
Helipads in São Paulo are seen by the boardroom and by the people.
The boardroom route by helipad in São Paulo wants to be seen.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses an em-dashed apposition and a precise purpose clause to layer the example into the argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for em-dashed apposition and a 'so that' clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which item is closest to the essay in register?
options: Cities are super unfair and we all know it, right?
The architecture of being noticed begins with frontage, sightline, and the depth of the set-back from the kerb.
Architecture begins with the things that begin architecture.
Frontage things start the noticing in cities, basically.
mcQuestions.explanation: B is concrete, precise, and uses parallel noun phrases — matching the essay's register.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the precise, parallel noun-phrase listing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence demonstrates a parenthetical aside via em-dashes?
options: The vestibule of a five-star hotel in Doha is more legible to a guest than the bus shelter outside is to a domestic worker waiting in heat — and the legibility is the point.
The vestibule, hotel five-star Doha legible, guest is more.
Doha hotel vestibule and bus shelter are both very legible.
Five-star hotels in Doha have vestibules that are legible.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses an em-dashed aside to deliver the rhetorical clincher ('and the legibility is the point').
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the em-dash before the clinching phrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which hedging device most closely matches the essay's tone?
options: one might argue
everyone definitely agrees
obviously and absolutely
no doubt whatsoever
mcQuestions.explanation: 'one might argue' is the essay's preferred soft-claim hedge, used near the close.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is closest in topic to the essay (architecture of visibility)?
options: Whose body the city registers is not chosen by the body but by the pavement.
Cities have pavements that everyone walks on.
Bodies and pavements are not connected in any way.
The pavement is a hard surface used by pedestrians.
mcQuestions.explanation: A captures the essay's core inversion: the pavement chooses the visible body, not vice versa.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line that inverts agency.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of one street, lobby, or plaza you walked through this week. Whose visibility did it reward, and whose did it quietly absorb into the background? What architectural decision made that happen?
